Mathematical Foundations of AI & ML
Unit 4: Neural Networks, Activations, and Convolutions

Prof. Dr. Philipp Pelz

FAU Erlangen-Nürnberg

Where we are in the triad

Just done (Unit 3):

  • Loss minimization, ERM, gradient descent.
  • Newton’s method, GLM/exponential-family unification.
  • Fixed basis functions \(\phi(x)\).

This unit:

  • The network learns its own basis functions.
  • Neurons → MLPs → convolutional layers.
  • Universal Approximation as the existence theorem.
  • Unit 5 turns these architectures into something we can train.

Frame the unit as the architectural half of deep learning. We define what a network is and why it has the expressive power it does. Unit 5 covers backprop and gradient flow — the algorithmic half. Unit 7 will cover generalization, Unit 9 representation learning. The CNN block at the end is foundational notation; ML-PC Unit 5 is the applied microstructure follow-up.

Learning outcomes

By the end of this unit, students can:

  • Trace the lineage from McCulloch–Pitts and the perceptron to the modern artificial neuron.
  • Prove that stacking linear layers without nonlinearities collapses to a single linear map.
  • Compare activation functions (sigmoid, tanh, ReLU, GeLU) by range, gradient, and saturation behavior.
  • State the Universal Approximation Theorem and articulate what it does not guarantee.
  • Define the 2-D cross-correlation operation and compute output sizes for given padding and stride.
  • Explain translation equivariance, weight sharing, and why CNNs are parameter-efficient for images.

Why neural networks?

  • Manual feature engineering is hard. For microstructures or multi-modal spectra, we don’t know the right \(\phi(x)\).
  • Hierarchical representation. Early layers learn simple features; later layers compose them into concepts (McClarren 2021).
  • Scalability. Performance improves with both sample count and feature dimension — a property polynomial bases lack.

The biological inspiration — and its limits

  • Biological neuron. Dendrites (inputs), soma (summation), axon (output), synapses (weights).
  • Modern view. Artificial neural networks are inspired by biology but are mathematical optimization tools, not models of cognition (Sandfeld et al. 2024).
  • The takeaway is the architecture motif: weighted sum, threshold, transmit. Everything else is engineering.

McCulloch–Pitts (1943) and Rosenblatt’s perceptron (1958)

McCulloch–Pitts

  • Binary inputs, binary threshold output.
  • Could implement AND, OR, NOT — but weights were fixed.
  • Establishes that networks of simple units can compute logic.

Perceptron

  • Added a learning rule — adjust weights from errors.
  • Convergence theorem. If data is linearly separable, the algorithm finds a separator in finite steps.
  • Single layer → still only linear decision boundaries.

The XOR crisis

graph LR
    X1((x1)) --> H1((h1))
    X1((x1)) --> H2((h2))
    X2((x2)) --> H1
    X2((x2)) --> H2
    H1 --> Y((y))
    H2 --> Y

  • Minsky & Papert (1969): a single-layer perceptron cannot solve XOR — the classes are not linearly separable.
  • The fix: hidden layers with non-linear activations.
  • Triggered the first AI winter; resolved by the multi-layer perceptron (MLP).

Interactive: fixing the XOR crisis

Use a hidden layer to warp space until the classes become linearly separable.

viewof showHidden = Inputs.toggle({label: "Warp to hidden-layer space", value: false})
  • XOR data: (0,0), (1,1) → 🔴; (0,1), (1,0) → 🔵.
  • In input space \((x_1, x_2)\) no single line separates the classes.
  • Hidden layer: \(h_1 = \text{ReLU}(x_1 - x_2)\), \(h_2 = \text{ReLU}(x_2 - x_1)\).
  • In \((h_1, h_2)\) the points are linearly separable.
xorOriginal = [
  {x: 0, y: 0, class: 0, label: "(0,0)"},
  {x: 1, y: 0, class: 1, label: "(1,0)"},
  {x: 0, y: 1, class: 1, label: "(0,1)"},
  {x: 1, y: 1, class: 0, label: "(1,1)"}
]

xorHiddenData = xorOriginal.map(d => ({
  x: Math.max(0, d.x - d.y),
  y: Math.max(0, d.y - d.x),
  class: d.class,
  label: d.label
}))

Plot.plot({
  x: {domain: [-0.5, 1.5], grid: true, label: showHidden ? "Hidden h₁" : "Input x₁"},
  y: {domain: [-0.5, 1.5], grid: true, label: showHidden ? "Hidden h₂" : "Input x₂"},
  color: {domain: [0, 1], range: ["#d62728", "#1f77b4"], legend: false},
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleX([0]),
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.dot(showHidden ? xorHiddenData : xorOriginal, {x: "x", y: "y", fill: "class", r: 10}),
    Plot.text(showHidden ? xorHiddenData : xorOriginal, {x: "x", y: "y", text: "label", dy: -20, fill: "white", fontSize: 16}),
    showHidden ? Plot.line([[-0.5, 1], [1.5, -1]], {stroke: "white", strokeWidth: 3, strokeDasharray: "4 4"}) : null
  ]
})

The artificial neuron

graph LR
    X1((x1)) -- W1 --> Sum[Σ]
    X2((x2)) -- W2 --> Sum
    dots[...] --> Sum
    XD((xD)) -- WD --> Sum
    B((1)) -- b --> Sum
    Sum --> Act[σ]
    Act --> Out((a))

  • Affine pre-activation: \(z = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b\).
  • Activation: \(a = \sigma(z)\).
  • Parameters. \(\mathbf{w}\) rotates the decision hyperplane; \(b\) shifts it off the origin.

Role of the bias term

  • Without \(b\), the hyperplane \(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} = 0\) must pass through the origin — a strong, unwanted constraint.
  • Geometrically, \(b\) provides one extra degree of freedom: a parallel translation.
  • Vectorized trick. Append a constant \(1\) to \(\mathbf{x}\) and absorb \(b\) into \(\mathbf{w}\) — algebra simplifies, semantics unchanged.

Interactive: the decision boundary

Watch how \((w_1, w_2)\) rotates the boundary and \(b\) shifts it.

viewof w1 = Inputs.range([-5, 5], {value: 2, step: 0.1, label: "Weight w₁"})
viewof w2 = Inputs.range([-5, 5], {value: 3, step: 0.1, label: "Weight w₂"})
viewof b_val = Inputs.range([-10, 10], {value: -2, step: 0.1, label: "Bias b"})

The boundary is \(z = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + b = 0\). The weight vector \((w_1, w_2)\) (red) is always perpendicular to it.

grid_data = {
  const pts = [];
  for (let x = -5; x <= 5; x += 0.25) {
    for (let y = -5; y <= 5; y += 0.25) {
      let z = w1*x + w2*y + b_val;
      pts.push({x: x, y: y, z: z > 0 ? "Positive (z>0)" : "Negative (z<=0)"});
    }
  }
  return pts;
}

Plot.plot({
  x: {domain: [-5, 5], grid: true, label: "x₁"},
  y: {domain: [-5, 5], grid: true, label: "x₂"},
  color: {domain: ["Positive (z>0)", "Negative (z<=0)"], range: ["#d62728", "#1f77b4"], legend: true},
  aspectRatio: 1,
  marks: [
    Plot.dot(grid_data, {x: "x", y: "y", fill: "z", r: 4, fillOpacity: 0.5}),
    Plot.ruleX([0], {strokeWidth: 2}),
    Plot.ruleY([0], {strokeWidth: 2}),
    Plot.arrow([{x: 0, y: 0}], {x1: 0, y1: 0, x2: w1, y2: w2, stroke: "red", strokeWidth: 3, headLength: 10}),
    Plot.text([{x: w1, y: w2, text: "w"}], {x: "x", y: "y", text: "text", dy: -10, fill: "red", fontSize: 20})
  ]
})

Why nonlinearity is non-negotiable

  • Composition of linear maps is linear. \(\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{B}\mathbf{x}) = (\mathbf{AB})\mathbf{x}\).
  • A 1000-layer purely linear network is mathematically a single linear regression.
  • Activations bend space — they let the network represent non-convex manifolds, learn disentangled features, and solve XOR-like problems.
  • Choice of activation is therefore a modeling decision, not a stylistic one.

Activation taxonomy

Classic (bounded)

  • Sigmoid \(\sigma(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z})\) — range \((0,1)\), probabilistic.
  • Tanh — range \((-1,1)\), zero-centered.
  • Softmax — multi-class probability (output layer only).

Modern (unbounded)

  • ReLU \(\max(0, z)\) — fast, sparse, can “die”.
  • Leaky / PReLU — small slope on the negative side.
  • GeLU — Gaussian-weighted; standard in Transformers.

Interactive: activation explorer

Compare \(f(z)\) (solid) and \(f'(z)\) (dashed). Vanishing gradients hide in plain sight.

viewof actName = Inputs.select(
  ["Sigmoid", "Tanh", "ReLU", "Leaky ReLU", "ELU", "GeLU"],
  {value: "Sigmoid", label: "Activation"}
)
viewof z_val = Inputs.range([-5, 5], {value: 0, step: 0.1, label: "Input z"})
  • Vanishing gradients. Sigmoid/Tanh: \(f'(z) \to 0\) for large \(|z|\).
  • Dying ReLU. Strict zero gradient for \(z < 0\) — the unit stops learning.
  • Why this matters is the central topic of Unit 5.
actFns = {
  return {
    "Sigmoid": {f: z => 1 / (1 + Math.exp(-z)), df: z => {let s = 1/(1+Math.exp(-z)); return s*(1-s);}},
    "Tanh": {f: z => Math.tanh(z), df: z => 1 - Math.pow(Math.tanh(z), 2)},
    "ReLU": {f: z => Math.max(0, z), df: z => z >= 0 ? 1 : 0},
    "Leaky ReLU": {f: z => z > 0 ? z : 0.1*z, df: z => z >= 0 ? 1 : 0.1},
    "ELU": {f: z => z > 0 ? z : 1.0*(Math.exp(z)-1), df: z => z >= 0 ? 1 : 1.0*Math.exp(z)},
    "GeLU": {
      f: z => z * 0.5 * (1 + Math.tanh(Math.sqrt(2 / Math.PI) * (z + 0.044715 * Math.pow(z, 3)))),
      df: z => {
        let h = Math.sqrt(2/Math.PI) * (z + 0.044715 * Math.pow(z, 3));
        let cdf = 0.5 * (1 + Math.tanh(h));
        let pdf = Math.exp(-0.5 * z * z) / Math.sqrt(2 * Math.PI);
        return cdf + z * pdf;
      }
    }
  };
}

actData = {
  const pts = [];
  for (let z = -5; z <= 5; z += 0.05) {
    pts.push({z: z, val: actFns[actName].f(z), type: "f(z) Value"});
    pts.push({z: z, val: actFns[actName].df(z), type: "f'(z) Gradient"});
  }
  return pts;
}

Plot.plot({
  x: {domain: [-5, 5], grid: true, label: "z (Input)"},
  y: {domain: [-2, 5], grid: true, label: "Output"},
  color: {domain: ["f(z) Value", "f'(z) Gradient"], range: ["#1f77b4", "#Eebb00"], legend: true},
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleX([0]),
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(actData.filter(d => d.type === "f(z) Value"), {x: "z", y: "val", stroke: "#1f77b4", strokeWidth: 4}),
    Plot.line(actData.filter(d => d.type === "f'(z) Gradient"), {x: "z", y: "val", stroke: "#Eebb00", strokeWidth: 3, strokeDasharray: "6 6"}),
    Plot.dot([{z: z_val, val: actFns[actName].f(z_val)}], {x: "z", y: "val", r: 6, fill: "#1f77b4"}),
    Plot.text([{z: z_val, val: actFns[actName].f(z_val), text: "f = " + actFns[actName].f(z_val).toFixed(2)}], {x: "z", y: "val", dy: -25, fill: "white", fontSize: 16}),
    Plot.text([{z: z_val, val: actFns[actName].df(z_val), text: "f' = " + actFns[actName].df(z_val).toFixed(2)}], {x: "z", y: "val", dy: 25, fill: "#Eebb00", fontSize: 16})
  ]
})

Activation cheat sheet

Activation Formula Range Gradient issue Use today
Sigmoid \(1/(1+e^{-z})\) \((0,1)\) saturates → vanishing binary output, gates
Tanh \(\tanh z\) \((-1,1)\) saturates RNN states
ReLU \(\max(0,z)\) \([0,\infty)\) dying ReLU hidden layers (default)
Leaky / PReLU \(\max(\alpha z, z)\) \(\mathbb{R}\) mild drop-in for ReLU
ELU piecewise \((-\alpha,\infty)\) smooth sometimes faster
GeLU \(z\,\Phi(z)\) \(\mathbb{R}\) mild Transformers
Softmax \(e^{z_j}/\sum_k e^{z_k}\) simplex — classification output

Multi-layer perceptron (MLP)

  • Also called fully connected or dense networks.
  • Every neuron in layer \(\ell\) is connected to every neuron in layer \(\ell-1\).
  • Vectorized layer: \(\mathbf{a}^{(\ell)} = \sigma\!\left(\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \mathbf{a}^{(\ell-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}\right)\).
  • Depth controls compositional complexity; width controls representational capacity per level.

Forward propagation

  1. Input: \(\mathbf{a}^{(0)} = \mathbf{x}\).
  2. For \(\ell = 1, \ldots, L\): \(\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)} = \mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \mathbf{a}^{(\ell-1)} + \mathbf{b}^{(\ell)}\), \(\mathbf{a}^{(\ell)} = \sigma(\mathbf{z}^{(\ell)})\).
  3. Output: \(\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{a}^{(L)}\).

Shape checklist (don’t skip this)

  • Input \(\mathbf{x}\) is \(D \times 1\); layer \(\ell\) has \(M_\ell\) neurons.
  • \(\mathbf{W}^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{M_\ell \times M_{\ell-1}}\), \(\mathbf{b}^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{M_\ell}\).
  • Batched: \(N\) samples → \(\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{D \times N}\), outputs \(\mathbf{A}^{(\ell)} \in \mathbb{R}^{M_\ell \times N}\).
  • Hardware reality: these are matrix–matrix multiplies; GPUs/TPUs run them embarrassingly in parallel.

Weight-space symmetries

  • Swap two hidden neurons (and their weights) — function unchanged.
  • Loss landscape is therefore highly redundant; many equivalent global minima.
  • Practical consequence: weights cannot be initialized to the same value, or every neuron in a layer learns the same feature.
  • Initialization schemes (Xavier, He) that preserve gradient variance are derived in Unit 5.

Universal Approximation Theorem (Cybenko, 1989)

  • A feed-forward net with one hidden layer and a non-polynomial activation can approximate any continuous function on a compact set to arbitrary precision.
  • What it guarantees. Existence of an arbitrarily-good approximator.
  • What it does not guarantee.
    • How many neurons are needed (could be exponential).
    • That SGD will find such weights.
    • That the solution will generalize beyond the training data.

Interactive: universal approximation

Three ReLU neurons composed by hand. Match the green target.

\(\hat{y} = \sum_{k=1}^{3} w_{\text{out},k}\,\text{ReLU}(x + b_k)\)

viewof w_out1 = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 1, step: 0.1, label: "w_out 1"})
viewof b1 = Inputs.range([-5, 5], {value: 2, step: 0.1, label: "bias 1"})

viewof w_out2 = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: -2, step: 0.1, label: "w_out 2"})
viewof b2 = Inputs.range([-5, 5], {value: 0, step: 0.1, label: "bias 2"})

viewof w_out3 = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 1, step: 0.1, label: "w_out 3"})
viewof b3 = Inputs.range([-5, 5], {value: -2, step: 0.1, label: "bias 3"})

By composing piecewise-linear ReLUs you can build any shape. Tweak the weights to track the green target.

uatData = {
  const pts = [];
  for (let x = -5; x <= 5; x += 0.05) {
    let r1 = w_out1 * Math.max(0, x + b1);
    let r2 = w_out2 * Math.max(0, x + b2);
    let r3 = w_out3 * Math.max(0, x + b3);
    let y_hat = r1 + r2 + r3;
    let y_target = Math.max(0, 2 - Math.abs(x));

    pts.push({x: x, y: r1, type: "Neuron 1"});
    pts.push({x: x, y: r2, type: "Neuron 2"});
    pts.push({x: x, y: r3, type: "Neuron 3"});
    pts.push({x: x, y: y_hat, type: "Network Output Sum"});
    pts.push({x: x, y: y_target, type: "Target Function"});
  }
  return pts;
}

Plot.plot({
  x: {domain: [-5, 5], grid: true, label: "x (Input)"},
  y: {domain: [-2, 4], grid: true, label: "y (Output)"},
  color: {
    domain: ["Neuron 1", "Neuron 2", "Neuron 3", "Network Output Sum", "Target Function"],
    range: ["#1f77b4", "#ff7f0e", "#9467bd", "white", "#2ca02c"],
    legend: true
  },
  marks: [
    Plot.ruleX([0]),
    Plot.ruleY([0]),
    Plot.line(uatData.filter(d => d.type.startsWith("Neuron")), {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "type", strokeDasharray: "2 2", strokeWidth: 2, strokeOpacity: 0.8}),
    Plot.line(uatData.filter(d => d.type === "Network Output Sum"), {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "white", strokeWidth: 4}),
    Plot.line(uatData.filter(d => d.type === "Target Function"), {x: "x", y: "y", stroke: "#2ca02c", strokeWidth: 4, strokeDasharray: "6 4"})
  ]
})

Expressivity vs. learnability

  • Expressivity. Can the architecture represent the solution? UAT: yes.
  • Learnability. Will SGD find it in reasonable time and data? Much harder, no general guarantee.
  • This gap is the entire reason the rest of the semester exists.

Why MLPs are the wrong tool for images

  • A \(256 \times 256\) RGB image has \(\sim 2 \times 10^5\) inputs. A first hidden layer of size 1000 needs \(\sim 2 \times 10^8\) weights — for one layer.
  • An MLP must re-learn the same edge detector at every spatial location independently. No weight sharing.
  • Flattening the image destroys spatial structure: pixel \((i,j)\) has no privileged relationship to its neighbors.
  • Two priors we want to bake in (Zhang et al. 2023):
    • Translation equivariance — a feature shifted in the input shifts identically in the output.
    • Locality — relevant patterns are localized in space.

From dense layer to convolution

  • Start with a fully connected layer \(h_{i,j} = \sum_{k,\ell} W_{i,j,k,\ell}\, x_{k,\ell} + b_{i,j}\) over a 2-D image.
  • Locality prior: zero out \(W\) whenever \(|k-i|\) or \(|\ell-j|\) exceeds \(\Delta\). Now each output depends only on a \(2\Delta+1\) patch.
  • Translation-equivariance prior: require \(W_{i,j,k,\ell} = V_{k-i,\,\ell-j}\) — the weights depend only on the relative offset.
  • Substituting both: \(h_{i,j} = \sum_{a,b} V_{a,b}\, x_{i+a,\,j+b}\). This is a convolution layer.

The 2-D cross-correlation operation

  • For input \(\mathbf{X}\) and kernel \(\mathbf{K}\) of size \(k_h \times k_w\): \[ \mathbf{Y}[i,j] = \sum_{a=0}^{k_h-1} \sum_{b=0}^{k_w-1} \mathbf{K}[a,b]\, \mathbf{X}[i+a,\, j+b]. \]
  • Naming nit. Deep learning calls this “convolution” but technically uses cross-correlation (no kernel flip). The flip is irrelevant because the kernel is learned.
  • Output size with input \(n_h \times n_w\) and kernel \(k_h \times k_w\), no padding, stride 1: \((n_h - k_h + 1) \times (n_w - k_w + 1)\).

Interactive: handcrafted kernels

Pick a \(3\times 3\) kernel and watch what it detects on a synthetic stripe pattern.

viewof kernelChoice = Inputs.select(
  ["Vertical edge (Sobel-x)", "Horizontal edge (Sobel-y)", "Blur (box)", "Sharpen", "Identity"],
  {value: "Vertical edge (Sobel-x)", label: "Kernel"}
)
  • Edge filters give large responses where intensity changes — this is what an early CNN layer learns by itself.
  • Blur kernels average; sharpen kernels amplify high frequencies.
  • A learned CNN combines hundreds of such filters in parallel (channels).
kernels = ({
  "Vertical edge (Sobel-x)": [[-1,0,1],[-2,0,2],[-1,0,1]],
  "Horizontal edge (Sobel-y)": [[-1,-2,-1],[0,0,0],[1,2,1]],
  "Blur (box)": [[1/9,1/9,1/9],[1/9,1/9,1/9],[1/9,1/9,1/9]],
  "Sharpen": [[0,-1,0],[-1,5,-1],[0,-1,0]],
  "Identity": [[0,0,0],[0,1,0],[0,0,0]]
})

inputImg = {
  const N = 32;
  const arr = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    for (let j = 0; j < N; j++) {
      // synthetic: bright disc + diagonal stripe
      const r = Math.hypot(i - 12, j - 12);
      const stripe = ((i + j) % 8 < 4) ? 0.4 : 0.0;
      const disc = r < 7 ? 1.0 : 0.0;
      arr.push({i: i, j: j, v: Math.min(1, disc + stripe)});
    }
  }
  return {N: N, arr: arr};
}

convolved = {
  const N = inputImg.N;
  const K = kernels[kernelChoice];
  const get = (i, j) => {
    if (i < 0 || j < 0 || i >= N || j >= N) return 0;
    return inputImg.arr[i*N + j].v;
  };
  const out = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < N-2; i++) {
    for (let j = 0; j < N-2; j++) {
      let s = 0;
      for (let a = 0; a < 3; a++)
        for (let b = 0; b < 3; b++)
          s += K[a][b] * get(i+a, j+b);
      out.push({i: i, j: j, v: s});
    }
  }
  return out;
}

Plot.plot({
  marginLeft: 40,
  width: 900,
  height: 420,
  color: {scheme: "viridis", legend: true},
  x: {axis: null},
  y: {axis: null, reverse: true},
  marks: [
    Plot.cell(inputImg.arr, {x: "j", y: "i", fill: "v", inset: 0.5}),
    Plot.text([{x: 16, y: -2, text: "Input image (32×32)"}], {x: "x", y: "y", text: "text", fill: "white", fontSize: 16})
  ]
})
Plot.plot({
  marginLeft: 40,
  width: 900,
  height: 420,
  color: {scheme: "magma", legend: true},
  x: {axis: null},
  y: {axis: null, reverse: true},
  marks: [
    Plot.cell(convolved, {x: "j", y: "i", fill: "v", inset: 0.5}),
    Plot.text([{x: 15, y: -2, text: "Output feature map"}], {x: "x", y: "y", text: "text", fill: "white", fontSize: 16})
  ]
})

Padding and stride

Padding — add \(p_h\), \(p_w\) rows/columns of zeros on each side.

  • Prevents shrinkage and edge information loss.
  • Common: \(p = (k-1)/2\) (“same” padding) so output matches input.

Stride — step size \(s_h\), \(s_w\) between kernel placements.

  • \(s>1\) downsamples — fewer outputs, larger receptive fields.
  • Replaces or complements pooling.

Output shape: \[ \left\lfloor \frac{n_h + 2p_h - k_h}{s_h} \right\rfloor + 1 \;\times\; \left\lfloor \frac{n_w + 2p_w - k_w}{s_w} \right\rfloor + 1. \] Memorize this formula — you will use it on every architecture diagram.

Multiple input and output channels

  • Input channels \(c_i\): e.g. RGB (\(c_i=3\)), or detector bands in a hyperspectral image.
  • Per-output-channel kernel has shape \(c_i \times k_h \times k_w\). Output is summed over \(c_i\).
  • Output channels \(c_o\): stack \(c_o\) such kernels — total kernel tensor \(\mathbf{K} \in \mathbb{R}^{c_o \times c_i \times k_h \times k_w}\).
  • Parameter count of one conv layer: \(c_o \cdot c_i \cdot k_h \cdot k_w\) + \(c_o\) biases — independent of image size.
  • \(1\times1\) convolutions mix channels at each pixel — cheap, used for projection and bottlenecks.

Why convolutions are parameter-efficient

  • A \(3\times 3\) conv with \(c_i = c_o = 64\): \(\sim 37\text{k}\) parameters, applied to any image size.
  • An equivalent dense layer on a \(224\times 224\times 64\) feature map: \(\sim 10^{11}\) parameters — five orders of magnitude more.
  • Weight sharing is what makes vision learnable from realistic dataset sizes.
  • It’s also the right inductive bias when the underlying physics is translation-invariant — true for most microscopy.

Pooling

  • Max pooling: \(\mathbf{Y}[i,j] = \max_{(a,b) \in \text{window}} \mathbf{X}[i+a, j+b]\).
  • Average pooling: replace \(\max\) with mean.
  • Why we pool.
    • Local translation invariance — small shifts of features don’t move the output.
    • Receptive field growth — each subsequent layer sees a larger region of the input.
    • Resolution reduction — fewer activations downstream.
  • Modern architectures sometimes replace pooling with strided convolutions; the principle (downsample to grow the receptive field) is the same.

LeNet — the canonical CNN

graph LR
    I[Input<br/>1×28×28] --> C1[Conv 5×5<br/>6 channels<br/>+ tanh]
    C1 --> P1[AvgPool 2×2]
    P1 --> C2[Conv 5×5<br/>16 channels<br/>+ tanh]
    C2 --> P2[AvgPool 2×2]
    P2 --> F1[Flatten]
    F1 --> D1[Dense 120<br/>+ tanh]
    D1 --> D2[Dense 84<br/>+ tanh]
    D2 --> O[Softmax 10]

  • LeCun et al., 1989/1998 — handwritten-digit classifier deployed for postal codes and bank checks.
  • The blueprint conv → activation → pool → … → flatten → dense → softmax is still the skeleton of modern image classifiers.
  • AlexNet (2012), VGG, ResNet are scaled, deepened, and refined LeNets.

Materials example: tabular property prediction

  • Input: 20 composition variables + 5 process parameters.
  • Architecture: 3-layer MLP with widths \([25, 100, 50, 1]\), ReLU hidden, linear output.
  • Task: predict fracture toughness.
  • An MLP is the right tool here — features are tabular, no spatial structure.

Materials example: image descriptor learning

  • Input: \(256 \times 256\) SEM micrograph.
  • Architecture: convolutional stack → MLP bottleneck → 10-D morphology vector.
  • Task: unsupervised microstructure descriptor for retrieval and clustering.
  • ML-PC Unit 5 builds the full pipeline; this unit gives you the layer vocabulary.

Materials example: spectral regression

  • Input: 1-D XPS signal.
  • Architecture: 1-D conv layers + Leaky ReLU (low-signal regions remain alive).
  • Task: quantify chemical states of constituent elements (Neuer et al. 2024).
  • Same convolution principle, applied along the energy axis instead of two spatial axes.

Common failure modes

  • No input normalization. Mixed scales (Temperature in \([0, 1000]\), Pressure in \([0, 1]\)) distort the loss landscape and stall learning.
  • Wrong output activation. ReLU on a target that can be negative; sigmoid on a non-probability.
  • All weights initialized identically. Every neuron in a layer learns the same feature.
  • Wrong layer for the data. MLP on raw images burns parameters; conv layer on tabular data is meaningless.

Mitigation checklist

  • Normalize inputs to mean 0, variance 1 (or per-channel for images).
  • Match the output activation to the physical range of the target.
  • Pick hidden activations with Unit 5’s gradient-flow analysis in mind (preview: ReLU-family for deep nets).
  • Use convolutional layers when the data has spatial/temporal structure and translation matters.

Mini concept quiz

  1. Why can’t a 2-layer linear network solve XOR?
  2. What does the Universal Approximation Theorem actually guarantee — and what does it not guarantee?
  3. Given input \(32 \times 32\), kernel \(5 \times 5\), padding 1, stride 2 — what’s the output spatial size?
  4. Why does weight sharing in a conv layer make CNNs sample-efficient compared to MLPs on images?

Lecture vs exercise split

  • Lecture. Foundations: neuron, MLP, activations, UAT, convolutional layer, pooling.
  • Exercise. Build a tiny MLP and a tiny CNN in NumPy/PyTorch; verify forward shapes; reproduce a \(3\times 3\) Sobel filter as a hand-set conv layer.

Unit summary

  • A neuron is an affine map followed by a nonlinearity. Composition of these is everything.
  • Without nonlinearity, depth buys nothing.
  • UAT promises expressivity; learnability is harder, and is the topic of Unit 5.
  • Convolutions encode locality and translation equivariance as architectural priors — the right inductive bias for images, microscopy, and most spatial physics data.
  • Pooling and stride trade resolution for receptive field, a defining move in every modern vision network.

References + transition

  • Read. Neuer Ch. 4.5.1–4.5.3 (neuron + activations); McClarren Ch. 5.1; D2L Ch. 7 (CNNs) (Zhang et al. 2023).
  • Next unit. Backpropagation and gradient flow — how the network actually learns, why some activations break deep training, and how initialization keeps gradients alive.
  • ML-PC Unit 5. Convolutional networks for microstructure analysis — applied counterpart of today’s CNN block.
McClarren, Ryan G. 2021. Machine Learning for Engineers: Using Data to Solve Problems for Physical Systems. Springer.
Neuer, Michael et al. 2024. Machine Learning for Engineers: Introduction to Physics-Informed, Explainable Learning Methods for AI in Engineering Applications. Springer Nature.
Sandfeld, Stefan et al. 2024. Materials Data Science. Springer.
Zhang, Aston, Zachary C. Lipton, Mu Li, and Alexander J. Smola. 2023. Dive into Deep Learning. Cambridge University Press. https://d2l.ai/chapter_convolutional-neural-networks/index.html.

Example notebook
